title: The formal subjunctive and irrealis 'were'
lesson: English preserves the subjunctive in a handful of high-register constructions. The mandative (present) subjunctive uses the bare base form of the verb after expressions of demand, recommendation, or necessity ('insist that he resign', 'essential that she be present'), with no -s in the third person and 'be'/'not be' rather than 'is'/'isn't'. The irrealis 'were' (past subjunctive) appears in hypotheticals ('if I were', 'I wish it were', 'as though he were'). Other fossilised survivals include 'lest' + subjunctive, formulae like 'be that as it may' and 'come what may', and inverted formal conditionals with 'should' ('Should you require...').

items.text: The committee insists that every applicant {1} interviewed in person before any offer is made.

blanks.hint: mandative subjunctive: bare base form, not 'is'

items.explanation: After verbs of demanding/insisting, the that-clause takes the base form regardless of subject, so 'be' (passive) rather than 'is'. The American norm strongly prefers the subjunctive here; the British 'should be' is a stylistic alternative.

items.text: It is imperative that the minister {1} from the post before the inquiry begins.

blanks.hint: third person, but no -s in the subjunctive

items.explanation: After 'it is imperative/essential/vital that', the mandative subjunctive uses the uninflected base form: 'the minister resign', never 'resigns'.

items.text: Her doctor recommended that she {1} alcohol entirely during the treatment.

blanks.hint: base form after 'recommend that'

items.explanation: 'Recommend/suggest/propose that' trigger the subjunctive: 'she avoid', not 'she avoids'. The lack of -s is the only visible marker with a regular verb.

items.text: The regulations demand that no member {1} permitted to vote twice.

blanks.hint: negative subjunctive: 'be', not 'is'

items.explanation: The subjunctive of 'be' is invariant: 'no member be permitted'. With negation it is 'that he not be', never 'that he isn't'.

items.text: If the proposal {1} to fail, the entire department would be restructured within the month.

blanks.hint: irrealis 'were' in a hypothetical 'if...were to'

items.explanation: 'Were to' introduces a remote or tentative future hypothesis. The irrealis 'were' is used for all persons in formal style; 'was to' is colloquial and avoided here.

items.text: I wish it {1} otherwise, but the facts leave us no room for optimism.

blanks.hint: 'I wish it ___' takes the past subjunctive

items.explanation: After 'wish', a counterfactual present takes 'were' for all subjects in careful English: 'I wish it were', not 'I wish it was'.

items.text: He carried himself as though he {1} the rightful heir to the entire estate.

blanks.hint: 'as though' + unreal situation = 'were'

items.explanation: 'As if/as though' describing an unreal or contrary-to-fact comparison takes the subjunctive 'were'. (When the situation is presented as possibly true, 'was' is acceptable, but the counterfactual reading here calls for 'were'.)

items.text: She spoke softly {1} the children sleeping in the next room be woken.

blanks.hint: archaic-formal 'for fear that'; followed by the subjunctive

items.explanation: 'Lest' means 'in order that...not' / 'for fear that' and governs the bare subjunctive: 'lest...be woken'. It belongs to elevated written register and needs no further negative.

items.text: The deadline is unworkable. {1} that as it may, we are contractually bound to meet it.

blanks.hint: fixed concessive formula; capitalised here at sentence start

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a frozen subjunctive idiom meaning 'even so / nevertheless', conceding a point before overriding it. The verb stays 'be'; capitalise it as it opens the new sentence.

items.text: {1} you require any further assistance, please do not hesitate to contact our office.

blanks.hint: inverted formal conditional replacing 'if you should'

items.explanation: Inverting 'should' to the front of the clause ('Should you require...') is a formal, often commercial, equivalent of 'If you should require...'. The subject and 'should' swap places and 'if' is dropped. Capitalised as it begins the sentence.

items.text: The court ruled that the contested clause {1} struck from the agreement forthwith.

blanks.hint: mandative subjunctive after a verb of ruling/ordering

items.explanation: Verbs of ordering and adjudicating ('rule/order/decree that') take the mandative subjunctive: 'the clause be struck', a passive base form rather than 'is struck' or 'was struck'.

items.text: Come what {1}, the festival will go ahead exactly as planned.

blanks.hint: fixed concessive subjunctive idiom

items.explanation: 'Come what may' is a fossilised subjunctive meaning 'whatever happens'. The inverted 'come' is itself a subjunctive; the phrase is invariable and emphatic.

items.text: The auditor proposed that the board {1} early in the new year to review the findings.

blanks.hint: base form after 'propose that', no -s

items.explanation: 'Propose that' triggers the mandative subjunctive, so the verb stays in the uninflected base form: 'the board reconvene', never 'reconvenes'. The missing third-person -s is the only overt marker with this regular verb.

items.text: {1} it to say that the experiment had to be repeated many times before anyone believed the result.

blanks.hint: fossilised subjunctive opener (= 'let it be enough to say'); bare base form, capitalised at the start

items.explanation: 'Suffice it to say' is a frozen subjunctive formula (= 'let it be enough to say') in which 'suffice' is itself a bare subjunctive used without a third-person -s; the rest of the sentence is ordinary indicative. The tested form is the subjunctive 'Suffice' that opens the idiom.

items.text: It is vital that the witness {1} pressured into changing her account under any circumstances.

blanks.hint: negative mandative subjunctive: 'not be', never 'isn't'

items.explanation: After 'it is vital/essential that', the mandative subjunctive uses the bare 'be', and negation precedes it directly: 'that the witness not be pressured', not 'isn't pressured' or 'should not be'. The negative subjunctive places 'not' before the uninflected 'be'.

items.text: {1} the agreement to collapse, both governments would face severe economic consequences.

blanks.hint: inverted formal conditional: 'Were ... to' replaces 'If ... were to'; capitalised at the start

items.explanation: Formal conditionals can drop 'if' and invert the subjunctive 'were' to the front: 'Were the agreement to collapse' = 'If the agreement were to collapse'. The irrealis 'were' is used for all subjects, and the clause opens the sentence so it is capitalised.

items.text: It is high time the authorities {1} this scandal seriously instead of issuing empty statements.

blanks.hint: 'it is (high) time' + past subjunctive for an overdue action

items.explanation: 'It is (high) time' is followed by the past subjunctive to mark something that is overdue: 'it is high time they took it seriously' (not 'take'). The past-tense form here is irrealis, signalling that the action ought already to be happening but is not.

items.text: It is essential that each delegate {1} a valid passport when registering at the conference desk.

blanks.hint: base form after 'it is essential that'; third person, no -s

items.explanation: 'It is essential that' triggers the mandative subjunctive, so the verb keeps its uninflected base form: 'each delegate present', never 'presents'. The absent third-person -s is the only overt marker with this regular verb.

items.text: Every entrant, {1} they amateur or professional, must abide by exactly the same rules.

blanks.hint: fronted subjunctive 'be it ... or'; here 'be they ... or'

items.explanation: The fronted subjunctive 'be it X or Y' (here 'be they amateur or professional') is a concessive formula meaning 'whether they are X or Y'. The inverted subjunctive 'be' opens the clause and stays invariant for all subjects.

items.text: {1} it from me to question the board's judgement, but the figures simply do not add up.

blanks.hint: fossilised inverted subjunctive (= 'it would be presumptuous of me'); capitalised at the start

items.explanation: 'Far be it from me to...' is a frozen inverted subjunctive (= 'I would never presume to...'), used to disclaim an intention before doing exactly that. The subjunctive 'be' is fronted before the subject 'it'; the phrase opens the sentence, so it is capitalised.

items.text: The loan was granted on condition that the company {1} its full accounts to the bank each quarter.

blanks.hint: mandative subjunctive after the conjunction 'on condition that'; no -s

items.explanation: Conditional conjunctions of stipulation — 'on condition that', 'provided that', 'lest', 'so that' (purpose) — can govern the mandative subjunctive, so the verb stays in the bare base form: 'the company submit', never 'submits'. The missing third-person -s is the only overt marker with this regular verb.

items.text: The shareholders rejected the proposal that the chairman {1} given a seat on the new board.

blanks.hint: subjunctive in a noun complement clause after 'the proposal that...'

items.explanation: Abstract nouns of demand or recommendation ('proposal', 'recommendation', 'request', 'requirement') take the mandative subjunctive in their that-complement, exactly like the related verbs: 'the proposal that the chairman be given...', a passive base form rather than 'is given' or 'was given'.

items.text: {1} I known how steep the path would be, I would never have agreed to lead the group.

blanks.hint: inverted third conditional dropping 'if': 'Had I known' = 'If I had known'; capitalised at the start

items.explanation: Formal counterfactual conditionals can drop 'if' and invert the auxiliary to the front: 'Had I known' = 'If I had known'. The past-perfect 'had' moves before the subject 'I', signalling an unreal past condition; the clause opens the sentence, so it is capitalised.
